revision.text:
'''Ujigami''' is the name given to the guardian [[god]] or spirit of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]].
{{Reli-stub}}

An '''ujigami''' is a guardian [[god]] or [[spirit]] of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. Most '''ujigami''' live in natural places such as a forrest or a lake. When people take the notice of Ujigami they build [[shrines]] in their honor.
[[category:Shinto]]

An '''ujigami''' (''氏神'') is a guardian god or spirit of a particular place in the Shinto religion of Japan. Ujigami are generally considered “group-centered” deities (whether the group is a family, clan, or nation) with whom the ujiko or “clan-child” are related on the basis of real of fictive blood relationships therefore unity is also dependent on these ties. All people from the same lineage or group form a religious community.In a household setting, those not related by blood are considered “adopted” and allowed to participate in religious activities, while clans refer to a territorial unit. In traditional Japan most people who lived in the same village or sub-village were related to each other. A person would have a lifelong unbreakable bond with his ujigami that was determined by birth and status, not voluntarily. Therefore the bond could not be broken or given up on the basis of individual choice. On a nation level the emperor was considered as the stand in for the kami-decended human representative, where each ''ujiko'' was then a citizen under the emperor. Warriors in particular relied on ujigami to reinforce their familial bonds, intensify their military alliances and to cement their control over lands and the peasants who worked the land. Individual warrior families publicly proclaimed their control over the land by establishing a religious shrine for the worship of their clan ancestor (''ujigami'') or local tutelary deity who would assume the same functions. Each male member of the household would establish a permanent link to the family’s ujigami or tutelary spirits though special coming of age ceremonies. == History == '''Origin:''' When the early medieval period of private estates were flourishing, estate owners began to set up shrines to gods (''kami'') whom they would venerate not as ancestral deities but as guardian deities (''Chinjugami''). When the estates later declined, the same term for protector deities (''Chinju'') was transferred to all manner of deity long venerated in the provinces. In the early Edo period, partly because of the veneration showed by successful shoguns, new importance was attached to ubusunagami (''protector of the birth place''). In time guardian deities (''chinjugami'') and ''ubusunagami'' came to be regarded as one and the same deity at the heart of the village communities and both came to be known by the generic term of ''ujigami''.
'''Today ujigami cults can be broadly classified into three types.'''

# “Village ujigami” ; each local resident is considered a “clan member” (''ujiko'') and participates in the worshiping of the god (kami).
# “Yashiki-Ujigami or Ie-Ujigami” ; enshrined in a small shrine (''hokora'') within the grounds of individual family dwellings.
# “Ikke-Ujigami or Maki-Ujigami” ; an intermediate position between the other two types, since it is worshiped by all member of an extended family (''ikke'') or local neighborhood grouping (''maki'').

Gedatsu-Kai followers in particular emphasized the significance of ujigami as a protector of all people who live in certain area (regardless of religion or place of birth). They acknowledged the ujigami as a local tutelary deity. Through it people receive life and thanks should be expressed regularly to the ujigami at the local Shinto shrine close to their residence. == References == {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217-52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Teeuwen, Mark, John Breen, and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New Ork: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. . *“Religion and Spirital Development: Japan.” Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 07 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122-27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[de:Ujigami]] [[es:Ujigami]] [[ja:氏神]] [[ro:Ujigami]]

revision.comment: big expansion: refs, stuff from my translation of the ja article; see talk
revision.text:
An {{nihongo|'''ujigami'''|氏神}} is a guardian [[god]] or [[spirit]] of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]].
== Usage == The ujigami was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=6&ved=0CDwQ6AEwBQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
== History == The ujigami is thought to have been believed in only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]]
[[de:Ujigami]] [[es:Ujigami]] [[ja:氏神]] [[ro:Ujigami]]

An {{nihongo|'''ujigami'''|氏神}} is a guardian [[god]] or [[spirit]] of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]].
== Usage == The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=6&ved=0CDwQ6AEwBQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
== History == The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been believed in only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=2&ved=0CCoQ6AEwAQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0521223555|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=4&ved=0CDIQ6AEwAw#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshipped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the Heian period, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''chinju''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''ubusunagami''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' were seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0415311799|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126&dq=Ubusunagami&hl=en&ei=1mlTTfDWKMH6lwek2ISNCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1&ved=0CCcQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Ubusunagami&f=false|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref>
The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />

Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=4&ved=0CDIQ6AEwAw#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the Heian period, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''ubusunagami''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126&dq=Ubusunagami&hl=en&ei=1mlTTfDWKMH6lwek2ISNCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1&ved=0CCcQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Ubusunagami&f=false|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref>
The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />

[[de:Ujigami]] [[es:Ujigami]] [[fa:اوجی‌گامی]] [[fr:Ujigami]] [[ja:氏神]] [[pl:Ujigami]] [[ro:Ujigami]]

An {{nihongo|'''ujigami'''|氏神}} is a guardian [[god]] or [[spirit]] of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]].
== Usage == The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=6&ved=0CDwQ6AEwBQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
== History == The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been believed in only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=2&ved=0CCoQ6AEwAQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=4&ved=0CDIQ6AEwAw#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the Heian period, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''ubusunagami''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126&dq=Ubusunagami&hl=en&ei=1mlTTfDWKMH6lwek2ISNCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1&ved=0CCcQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Ubusunagami&f=false|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref>
The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />
== Notes == {{reflist}}
==References== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217-52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New Ork: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *“Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan.” Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 07 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122-27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}}

Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126&dq=Ubusunagami&hl=en&ei=1mlTTfDWKMH6lwek2ISNCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1&ved=0CCcQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Ubusunagami&f=false|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref>
The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />

{{Use dmy dates|date=January 2013}} An {{nihongo|'''ujigami'''|氏神}} is a guardian [[god]] or [[spirit]] of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]].
The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=6&ved=0CDwQ6AEwBQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
== History == The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been believed in only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=2&ved=0CCoQ6AEwAQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=4&ved=0CDIQ6AEwAw#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the Heian period, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''ubusunagami''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=http://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126&dq=Ubusunagami&hl=en&ei=1mlTTfDWKMH6lwek2ISNCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1&ved=0CCcQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Ubusunagami&f=false|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref>
The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />

{{Use dmy dates|date=January 2013}} An {{nihongo|'''ujigami'''|氏神}} is a guardian [[god]] or [[spirit]] of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]].
The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=6&ved=0CDwQ6AEwBQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|Ujigami shrine in Uji]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been believed in only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=2&ved=0CCoQ6AEwAQ#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388&dq=ujigami&hl=en&ei=AW1TTe_vA8GblgfWpfHfCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=4&ved=0CDIQ6AEwAw#v=onepage&q=ujigami&f=false}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the Heian period, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''ubusunagami''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126&dq=Ubusunagami&hl=en&ei=1mlTTfDWKMH6lwek2ISNCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1&ved=0CCcQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Ubusunagami&f=false|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref>
The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />
==See also== * [[Uji (clan)]]
== References == {{reflist}}
==Further reading== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217-52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122-27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}}

Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126&dq=Ubusunagami&hl=en&ei=1mlTTfDWKMH6lwek2ISNCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1&ved=0CCcQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Ubusunagami&f=false|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref>
The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />

Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last=Teeuwen|first=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126&dq=Ubusunagami&hl=en&ei=1mlTTfDWKMH6lwek2ISNCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1&ved=0CCcQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Ubusunagami&f=false|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref>
The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto minor deity}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "Clan Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} is a guardian [[god]] or spirit of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the Heian period, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujigami arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref>
The ritual of visiting a palace and entering a parishioner is not always the same, but as mentioned above, after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of origin disappeared, visiting a palace often means entering a parishioner. Therefore, it is common to be awarded a proof of childhood card when visiting a shrine. On the other hand, those who are not of a family who worship the Ujigami or engage in festivals for generations often just customarily visit the shrine and often do not have the consciousness or awareness of the Ujigami.
In addition, there are many cases in which a ritual of entering a parishioner is performed again for the Ujigami of the married family due to the marriage or marriage. Recently, the number of people engaged in festivals is aging, and the number of children participating in the festival is decreasing. In many cases, it is only paid and offered throughout the house.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]]
== References == {{reflist}}
==Further reading== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217–52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}}
{{Authority control}} [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]]

{{Short description|Shinto minor deity}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "Clan Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} is a guardian [[god]] or spirit of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>

The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the Heian period, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujigami arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref>
The ritual of visiting a palace and entering a parishioner is not always the same, but as mentioned above, after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of origin disappeared, visiting a palace often means entering a parishioner. Therefore, it is common to be awarded a proof of childhood card when visiting a shrine. On the other hand, those who are not of a family who worship the Ujigami or engage in festivals for generations often just customarily visit the shrine and often do not have the consciousness or awareness of the Ujigami.
In addition, there are many cases in which a ritual of entering a parishioner is performed again for the Ujigami of the married family due to the marriage or marriage. Recently, the number of people engaged in festivals is aging, and the number of children participating in the festival is decreasing. In many cases, it is only paid and offered throughout the house.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]
== References == {{reflist}}
==Further reading== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217–52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}}

{{Short description|Shinto minor deity}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "Clan Deity/Divinity/Spirit"}} is a guardian [[god]] or spirit of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the Heian period, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" />
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujigami arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref>
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the [[Heian period]], the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]
== References == {{reflist}}
==Further reading== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217–52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}}

{{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the [[Heian period]], the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]

{{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the [[Heian period]], the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]

{{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the [[Heian period]], the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]

New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. {{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the [[Heian period]], the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]

New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. {{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the [[Heian period]], the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]

New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. {{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the [[Heian period]], the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015年、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]

Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. {{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|[[wiktionary:氏神|氏神]], lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>

The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|, lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] [[religion]] of [[Japan]]. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in [[Uji]]]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the [[Heian period]], the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''[[chinjusha|chinju]]''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015th year 、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]
== References == {{reflist}}
==Further reading== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217–52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}} ]]]]
]]]] {{Shinto shrine}}

=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Hitogami]]
