==Further reading== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217–52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}} ]]]]
]]]] <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->

In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|WD=Q13479654|lt=chinju}}''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015th year 、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.

New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}} ]]]]

An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|, lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|WD=Q13479654|lt=chinju}}''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015th year 、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.

New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|WD=Q13479654|lt=chinju}}''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015th year 、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * {{ill|Hitogami|en|Hitogami|WD=Q11376147|lt=Hitogami}}
== References == {{reflist}}
==Further reading== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217–52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}} ]]]]
]]]] <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->

{{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|, lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji}}]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|WD=Q13479654|lt=chinju}}''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015th year 、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.

The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|WD=Q13479654|lt=chinju|1=Chinjusha}}''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015th year 、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * {{ill|Hitogami|en|Hitogami|WD=Q11376147|lt=Hitogami|1=Hitogami}}

Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}} ]]]]

{{Short description|Shinto guardian god of a particular place}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2022}}
An {{nihongo|'''''ujigami'''''|, lit. "clan deity/divinity/spirit"}} is a guardian ''[[kami]]'' of a particular place in the [[Shinto]] {{ill|Religion|en|Religion|WD=Q9174|lt=Religion|1=Religion}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>

The ''ujigami'' was prayed to for a number of reasons, including protection from sickness, success in endeavors, and good harvests.<ref>{{cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|title=Japan, an attempt at interpretation|year=1913|publisher=Macmillan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WZ9DAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA96}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Ujigami shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Ujigami Shrine]] in {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|WD=Q326956|lt=Uji|1=Uji}}]] The ''ujigami'' is thought to have been more important only since the eighth century.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encounter or syncretism. The initial growth of Japanese Buddhism|first=J. H. |last=Kamstra|year=1967|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NRsVAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA101}}</ref>
In its current form, the term ''ujigami'' is used to describe several other types of Shinto deities. Originally, the term ''ujigami'' referred to a family god.<ref name="cambridge">{{cite book|last=Hall|first=John Whitney|title=The Cambridge History of Japan: Early modern Japan|year=1991|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=0-521-22355-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ycdGHcKLcd8C&pg=PA388}}</ref> It is believed that, at first, these deities were worshiped at temporary altars.<ref name="cambridge" /> After the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the [[Shōen|Japanese manorial system]] was established and nobles, warriors and temples had their own private land, the family-based society fell out of use, and belief in ujigami diminished. In turn, the lords of the manors began to pray to the deities to protect their land. These guardian deities were referred to as {{nihongo|''{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|WD=Q13479654|lt=chinju|1=Chinjusha}}''|鎮守}}. In the Muromachi period the manorial system declined, and so the guardian deities were enshrined along with the ''ujigami''. An {{nihongo|''[[ubusunagami]]''|産土神}} is a god of the land of one's birth. Over time, the ''ubusunagami'' and ''chinju'' came to be seen as the heart of the community, and were eventually referred to as ''ujigami''.<ref name="shinto-history">{{cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|title=Shinto, a short history|year=2003|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=0-415-31179-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Fv45tBW4hUC&pg=PA126|first2=John|last2=Breen|first3=Nobutaka|last3=Inoue|first4=Satoshi|last4=Itō}}</ref> ==Ujiko== The term {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}} is used to describe a person who worships an ujigami.<ref name="cambridge" /><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Moriarty |first=Elizabeth |date=1972 |title=The Communitarian Aspect of Shinto Matsuri |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177490 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=91–140 |doi=10.2307/1177490 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
Usually, the relationship between Ujigami and Ujiko arises from entering the Ujigami at the birthplace of the Ujigami or at a local shrine. In many cases, the [[Ubusunagami]] is often used as the Ujigami, just as the shrine visit is performed on the Ubusunagami, the god of the land where he was born. <ref>小池康寿 『日本人なら知っておきたい正しい家相の本』 プレジデント社、2015th year 、90頁。</ref><ref name=":0" />
The custom of visiting a shrine and becoming a parishioner varies, but in many cases, it involves visiting a shrine after the distinction between a god of origin and a god of worship has diminished. Upon visiting a shrine, it is common to receive a proof of childhood card, indicating one's status as a parishioner. However, for those who do not have a familial connection to the worship of the Ujigami, or have not participated in festivals for generations, visiting the shrine may simply be a customary act without a strong conscious understanding of the Ujigami.
Additionally, the ritual of becoming a parishioner may be performed again following a marriage, to align with the Ujigami of the spouse's family. In recent years, the aging of festival participants and a decrease in children participating in festivals has been observed. In many cases, the offering of worship is limited to within the household.
=={{Anchor|Shrine}}Ujigami shrine== An ujigami shrine (氏社, Ujisha, Ujiyashiro) is a shrine dedicated to an Ujigami. Such shrines used to be limited to certain clan groups but now they are organized on a regional level.
==See also== * [[Chinjugami]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Sorei]] * [[Ubusunagami]] * [[Uji (clan)]] * {{ill|Hitogami|en|Hitogami|WD=Q11376147|lt=Hitogami|1=Hitogami}}
== References == {{reflist}}
==Further reading== {{refbegin}} *Hambrick, Charles H. "Tradition and Modernity in the New Religious Movements of Japan." Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1 (1974): 217–52. JSTOR. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *[[Mark Teeuwen|Teeuwen, Mark]], [[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]], and Ito Satoshi. "Shinto and the Populace: the Spread of Ritual and Teachings." Shinto, a Short History. New York: New York Taylor & Francis, 2003. 126. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. *Hiroshi, Iwai. "Kami in Folk Religion : Ujigami." Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home. Kokugakuin University, 13 Mar. 2005. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. <http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=231>. *"Religion and Spiritual Development: Japan." Martial Arts of the World. Santa Barabara: ABC-CLIO, 2001. Credo Reference. Web. 7 October 2010 *Earhart, Bryon H. "A Branch Meeting in Suburban Tokyo: "I" Branch." Gedatsu-Kai and Religion in Contemporary Japan: Returning to the Center. Bloomington Indiana UP, 1989. 122–27. NetLibrary. Web. 21 Sept. 2010. {{refend}} ]]]]
]]]] <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->
